title: Station Support at Victoria Station
transcript: You're going to hear a radio program. You will hear the program twice. First, read items 46 to 55. After you've listened to the program, decide if the statement for each of the items 46 to 55 is true or not true. Mark your answer on the answer sheet. Now you have one minute to read the statements. First, read the statement for each of the items 46 to 55. After you've listened to the program, decide if the statement for each of the items 46 to 55 is true or not true. Mark your answer on the answer sheet. You are going to hear a report about station support, a service at Victoria train station helping passengers in trouble. Sarah Jenkins, a team leader, is telling a reporter about the station support. about her daily tasks and the passengers she assists. It is a cold Friday evening at London Victoria station. We are in the small office of station support, a charity service for rail passengers. The night has just started, but the team is already busy. Sitting on a plastic chair is Maria, a 19-year-old student from Spain. She has been crying for 20 minutes. Sarah Jenkins, who has worked here for 6 years, hands her a cup of tea. Maria arrived in London for a weekend trip, but she left her backpack on the train from the airport. It contained her passport, her money, and the address of her hostel. Sarah is trying to calm her down, but it is difficult. Maria speaks very little English and she is panicked. Cases like Maria's are very common. Sarah explains that many young tourists underestimate how busy London stations can be. Fortunately for Maria, the cleaning staff found her bag on the train 10 minutes ago. Sarah has just received the call. However, not everyone is so lucky. Last year, station support helped over 3,000 people. Most of them had genuinely lost their tickets or missed their last connection home. One memorable case was Arthur, a pensioner from Scotland. He fell asleep in the waiting room and woke up after the station had closed for the night. The security guards brought him to us. We managed to contact his daughter, but she lived 3 hours away and couldn't collect him until the morning. We stayed with Arthur all night, playing cards and talking about his grandchildren, but sadly, some people try to take advantage of our kindness. We get people coming in saying they were robbed, hoping we will simply hand over cash for a new ticket. We have a strict policy. We never give money directly to passengers. Instead, we can issue a special voucher for a ticket if the police confirm a crime has happened, or we lend them a mobile phone to call relatives who can pay by credit card over the phone. Station support is unique in the capital. While the police patrol the station, they don't have the time to look after stranded passengers. Our funding comes partly from the rail companies, but mostly from public bucket collections and charity events. This money pays for our office rent and the refreshments we offer, like tea, coffee, and biscuits. The staff, however, are mostly volunteers like me, although we do have two paid managers. Despite our best efforts, we can't satisfy everyone. I remember a businessman who missed his train to Manchester because he was eating dinner in a restaurant. He came to our office and demanded that we pay for a taxi to take him all the way to Manchester, which would cost hundreds of pounds. When I told him we could only offer him a warm seat and a timetable for the morning train, he was absolutely furious. He told me he would complain to the government. It just shows that while we can help with disasters, we cannot fix bad planning. Now listen to the radio program again. You are going to hear a report about Station Support, a service at Victoria Train Station helping passengers in trouble. Sarah Jenkins, a team leader, is telling a reporter about her daily tasks and the passengers she assists. It is a cold Friday evening at London Victoria Station. We are in the small office of Station Support, a charity service for rail passengers. The night has just started, but the team is already busy. Sitting on a plastic chair is Maria, a 19-year-old student from Spain. She has been crying for 20 minutes. Sarah Jenkins, who has worked here for six years, hands her a cup of tea. Maria arrived in London for a weekend trip, but she left her backpack on the train from the airport. It contained her passport, her money, and the address of her hostel. Sarah is trying to calm her down, but it is difficult. Maria speaks very little English and she is panicked. Cases like Maria's are very common. Sarah explains that many young tourists underestimate how busy London's stations can be. Fortunately for Maria, the cleaning staff found her bag on the train 10 minutes ago. Sarah has just received the call. However, not everyone is so lucky. Last year, Station Support helped over 3,000 people. Most of them had genuinely lost their tickets or missed their last connection home. One memorable case was Arthur, a pensioner from Scotland. He fell asleep in the waiting room and woke up after the station had closed for the night. The security guards brought him to us. We managed to contact his daughter, but she lived three hours away and couldn't collect him until the morning. We stayed with Arthur all night, playing cards and talking about his grandchildren. But sadly, some people try to take advantage of our kindness. We get people coming in saying they were robbed, hoping we will simply hand over cash for a new ticket. We have a strict policy. We never give money directly to passengers. Instead, we can issue a special voucher for a ticket if the police confirm a crime has happened, or we lend them a mobile phone to call relatives who can pay by credit card over the phone. Station Support is unique in the capital. While the police patrol the station, they don't have the time to look after stranded passengers. Our funding comes partly from the rail companies, but mostly from public bucket collections and charity events. This money pays for our office rent and the refreshments we offer, like tea, coffee, and biscuits. The staff, however, are mostly volunteers like me, although we do have two paid managers. Despite our best efforts, we can't satisfy everyone. I remember a businessman who missed his train to Manchester because he was eating dinner in a restaurant. He came to our office and demanded that we pay for a taxi to take him all the way to Manchester, which would cost hundreds of pounds. When I told him we could only offer him a warm seat and a timetable for the morning train, he was absolutely furious. He told me he would complain to the government. It just shows that while we can help with disasters, we cannot fix bad planning. Now you have one minute to check your answers again. For more UN videos visit www.un.org

statements.statement: The report takes place at a railway station in London.
statements.audio_transcript: It is a cold Friday evening at London Victoria Station. We are in the small office of Station Support, a charity service for rail passengers.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio heißt es: «Es ist ein kalter Freitagabend an der London Victoria Station.» Victoria Station ist ein Bahnhof in London.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio states 'It is a cold Friday evening at London Victoria Station.' Victoria Station is a railway station in London.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يقول التسجيل 'إنه مساء جمعة بارد في محطة لندن فيكتوريا.' محطة فيكتوريا هي محطة قطارات في لندن.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio dit : 'C'est un vendredi soir froid à la gare de London Victoria.' Victoria Station est une gare ferroviaire à Londres.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio dice: 'Es un viernes frío por la noche en la estación London Victoria.' Victoria Station es una estación de tren en Londres.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится: 'Холодный пятничный вечер на станции Лондон Виктория.' Виктория — это железнодорожная станция в Лондоне.

statements.statement: Maria was upset because she forgot her backpack on a train.
statements.audio_transcript: Sitting on a plastic chair is Maria, a 19-year-old student from Spain. She has been crying for 20 minutes. Maria arrived in London for a weekend trip, but she left her backpack on the train from the airport.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio heißt es, Maria «weint seit 20 Minuten» und «hat ihren Rucksack im Zug vom Flughafen vergessen». Sie ist aufgelöst, weil sie ihren Rucksack im Zug gelassen hat.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio says Maria 'has been crying for 20 minutes' and 'she left her backpack on the train from the airport'. She is upset because she forgot her backpack on the train.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يقول التسجيل إن ماريا 'تبكي منذ 20 دقيقة' و'تركت حقيبتها في القطار من المطار'. هي منزعجة لأنها نسيت حقيبتها في القطار.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio dit que Maria 'pleure depuis 20 minutes' et qu'elle 'a laissé son sac à dos dans le train de l'aéroport'. Elle est bouleversée car elle a oublié son sac.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio dice que María 'lleva 20 minutos llorando' y 'dejó su mochila en el tren del aeropuerto'. Está disgustada porque olvidó su mochila en el tren.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что Мария 'плачет уже 20 минут' и 'оставила рюкзак в поезде из аэропорта'. Она расстроена, потому что забыла рюкзак в поезде.

statements.statement: Sarah Jenkins was unable to communicate with Maria because Maria speaks no English.
statements.audio_transcript: Sarah is trying to calm her down, but it is difficult. Maria speaks very little English and she is panicked.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Im Audio heißt es, Maria spreche «sehr wenig Englisch», nicht gar kein Englisch. Außerdem versucht Sarah sie zu beruhigen — Kommunikation war schwierig, aber nicht unmöglich.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. The audio says Maria 'speaks very little English', not no English at all. Sarah is trying to calm her down, so communication is difficult but not impossible.
explanation.ar: خطأ. يقول التسجيل إن ماريا 'تتحدث القليل جداً من الإنجليزية' وليس أنها لا تتحدثها إطلاقاً. التواصل صعب لكنه ليس مستحيلاً.
explanation.fr: FAUX. L'audio dit que Maria 'parle très peu anglais', pas qu'elle ne parle pas anglais du tout. La communication est difficile mais pas impossible.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. El audio dice que María 'habla muy poco inglés', no que no hable nada de inglés. La comunicación es difícil pero no imposible.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что Мария 'говорит очень мало по-английски', а не что она совсем не говорит. Общение затруднено, но не невозможно.

statements.statement: The cleaners found Maria's belongings shortly after she arrived at the office.
statements.audio_transcript: Fortunately for Maria, the cleaning staff found her bag on the train 10 minutes ago. Sarah has just received the call.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio heißt es, das Reinigungspersonal habe Marias Tasche «vor 10 Minuten» gefunden. Da Maria seit 20 Minuten weint, wurde die Tasche kurz nach ihrer Ankunft gefunden.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio says the cleaning staff found her bag '10 minutes ago'. Since Maria has been crying for 20 minutes, the bag was found shortly after she arrived at the office.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يقول التسجيل إن طاقم التنظيف وجد حقيبتها 'قبل 10 دقائق'. بما أن ماريا تبكي منذ 20 دقيقة، فقد وُجدت الحقيبة بعد وصولها بوقت قصير.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio dit que le personnel de nettoyage a trouvé son sac 'il y a 10 minutes'. Puisque Maria pleure depuis 20 minutes, le sac a été trouvé peu après son arrivée.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio dice que el personal de limpieza encontró su bolsa 'hace 10 minutos'. Como María lleva 20 minutos llorando, la bolsa se encontró poco después de su llegada.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что уборщики нашли сумку '10 минут назад'. Поскольку Мария плачет уже 20 минут, сумка была найдена вскоре после её прихода.

statements.statement: Arthur needed help because his train ticket had been stolen while he slept.
statements.audio_transcript: One memorable case was Arthur, a pensioner from Scotland. He fell asleep in the waiting room and woke up after the station had closed for the night. The security guards brought him to us.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Arthurs Problem war nicht ein gestohlenes Ticket. Im Audio heißt es, er sei «im Wartezimmer eingeschlafen und aufgewacht, nachdem die Station für die Nacht geschlossen hatte». Er saß einfach fest.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. Arthur's problem was not a stolen ticket. The audio says he 'fell asleep in the waiting room and woke up after the station had closed for the night'. He was simply stranded.
explanation.ar: خطأ. لم تكن مشكلة آرثر تذكرة مسروقة. يقول التسجيل إنه 'نام في غرفة الانتظار واستيقظ بعد إغلاق المحطة ليلاً'. كان عالقاً فحسب.
explanation.fr: FAUX. Le problème d'Arthur n'était pas un billet volé. L'audio dit qu'il 's'est endormi dans la salle d'attente et s'est réveillé après la fermeture de la gare'. Il était simplement bloqué.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. El problema de Arthur no fue un billete robado. El audio dice que 'se quedó dormido en la sala de espera y se despertó después de que la estación cerró por la noche'. Simplemente quedó atrapado.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. Проблемой Артура был не украденный билет. В аудио говорится, что он 'уснул в зале ожидания и проснулся после закрытия станции на ночь'. Он просто застрял.

statements.statement: Arthur's daughter came to pick him up from the station immediately.
statements.audio_transcript: We managed to contact his daughter, but she lived three hours away and couldn't collect him until the morning. We stayed with Arthur all night, playing cards and talking about his grandchildren.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Im Audio heißt es, die Tochter «wohnte drei Stunden entfernt und konnte ihn erst am Morgen abholen». Die Mitarbeiter blieben die ganze Nacht bei Arthur — sie kam also nicht sofort.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. The audio says his daughter 'lived three hours away and couldn't collect him until the morning'. The staff stayed with Arthur all night, so she did not come immediately.
explanation.ar: خطأ. يقول التسجيل إن ابنته 'تعيش على بُعد ثلاث ساعات ولم تستطع اصطحابه حتى الصباح'. بقي الموظفون مع آرثر طوال الليل.
explanation.fr: FAUX. L'audio dit que sa fille 'habitait à trois heures de route et ne pouvait pas venir le chercher avant le matin'. Le personnel est resté avec Arthur toute la nuit.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. El audio dice que su hija 'vivía a tres horas de distancia y no podía recogerlo hasta la mañana'. El personal se quedó con Arthur toda la noche.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что дочь 'жила в трёх часах езды и не могла забрать его до утра'. Сотрудники остались с Артуром на всю ночь.

statements.statement: Station Support refuses to give cash to the people who come to them.
statements.audio_transcript: We have a strict policy. We never give money directly to passengers. Instead, we can issue a special voucher for a ticket if the police confirm a crime has happened, or we lend them a mobile phone to call relatives who can pay by credit card over the phone.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Sarah sagt ausdrücklich: «Wir haben eine strenge Regel. Wir geben Reisenden nie direkt Geld.» Stattdessen bieten sie Gutscheine oder Telefonhilfe an.
explanation.en: CORRECT. Sarah explicitly says 'We have a strict policy. We never give money directly to passengers.' Instead they offer vouchers or phone assistance.
explanation.ar: صحيح. تقول سارة صراحةً: 'لدينا سياسة صارمة. لا نعطي المال مباشرة للمسافرين.' بدلاً من ذلك يقدمون قسائم أو مساعدة هاتفية.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. Sarah dit explicitement : 'Nous avons une politique stricte. Nous ne donnons jamais d'argent directement aux passagers.' Ils offrent des bons ou une aide téléphonique.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. Sarah dice explícitamente: 'Tenemos una política estricta. Nunca damos dinero directamente a los pasajeros.' En su lugar ofrecen vales o ayuda telefónica.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. Сара прямо говорит: 'У нас строгая политика. Мы никогда не даём деньги пассажирам напрямую.' Вместо этого предлагают ваучеры или помощь по телефону.

statements.statement: The organization is funded entirely by the government and police.
statements.audio_transcript: Our funding comes partly from the rail companies, but mostly from public bucket collections and charity events. This money pays for our office rent and the refreshments we offer, like tea, coffee, and biscuits.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Im Audio heißt es, die Finanzierung komme «teilweise von Bahnunternehmen, aber hauptsächlich von öffentlichen Spendensammlungen und Wohltätigkeitsveranstaltungen». Weder Regierung noch Polizei werden als Geldgeber genannt.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. The audio says funding 'comes partly from the rail companies, but mostly from public bucket collections and charity events'. Neither the government nor the police are mentioned as funders.
explanation.ar: خطأ. يقول التسجيل إن التمويل 'يأتي جزئياً من شركات السكك الحديدية، لكن في الغالب من جمع التبرعات العامة والفعاليات الخيرية'. لم تُذكر الحكومة ولا الشرطة كممولين.
explanation.fr: FAUX. L'audio dit que le financement 'vient en partie des compagnies ferroviaires, mais surtout des collectes publiques et d'événements caritatifs'. Ni le gouvernement ni la police ne sont mentionnés.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. El audio dice que la financiación 'proviene en parte de las compañías ferroviarias, pero principalmente de colectas públicas y eventos benéficos'. No se menciona al gobierno ni a la policía.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что финансирование 'частично поступает от железнодорожных компаний, но в основном от публичных сборов и благотворительных мероприятий'. Ни правительство, ни полиция не упоминаются как спонсоры.

statements.statement: Most of the people working for Station Support are not paid for their work.
statements.audio_transcript: The staff, however, are mostly volunteers like me, although we do have two paid managers.

explanation.de: RICHTIG. Im Audio heißt es, die Mitarbeiter seien «größtenteils Freiwillige», obwohl es «zwei bezahlte Manager» gebe. Die meisten arbeiten also unbezahlt.
explanation.en: CORRECT. The audio says the staff are 'mostly volunteers like me, although we do have two paid managers'. Most workers are unpaid volunteers.
explanation.ar: صحيح. يقول التسجيل إن الموظفين 'معظمهم متطوعون مثلي، رغم أن لدينا مديرين مدفوعي الأجر'. معظم العاملين متطوعون غير مدفوعين.
explanation.fr: CORRECT. L'audio dit que le personnel est 'composé principalement de bénévoles comme moi, bien que nous ayons deux managers salariés'. La plupart travaillent bénévolement.
explanation.es: CORRECTO. El audio dice que el personal 'son en su mayoría voluntarios como yo, aunque tenemos dos gerentes pagados'. La mayoría trabaja sin remuneración.
explanation.ru: ПРАВИЛЬНО. В аудио говорится, что сотрудники 'в основном волонтёры, как я, хотя у нас есть два оплачиваемых менеджера'. Большинство работают бесплатно.

statements.statement: The businessman was angry because the service refused to buy him a plane ticket.
statements.audio_transcript: He came to our office and demanded that we pay for a taxi to take him all the way to Manchester, which would cost hundreds of pounds. When I told him we could only offer him a warm seat and a timetable for the morning train, he was absolutely furious.

explanation.de: FALSCH. Der Geschäftsmann wollte kein Flugticket. Im Audio heißt es, er habe verlangt, dass sie «ein Taxi nach Manchester bezahlen». Er war wütend, weil sie ihm nur «einen warmen Sitzplatz und einen Fahrplan» anboten.
explanation.en: INCORRECT. The businessman did not want a plane ticket. The audio says he 'demanded that we pay for a taxi to take him all the way to Manchester'. He was angry because they could only offer 'a warm seat and a timetable for the morning train'.
explanation.ar: خطأ. لم يطلب رجل الأعمال تذكرة طائرة. يقول التسجيل إنه 'طلب أن ندفع ثمن سيارة أجرة لتوصيله إلى مانشستر'. غضب لأنهم عرضوا عليه فقط 'مقعداً دافئاً وجدول مواعيد'.
explanation.fr: FAUX. L'homme d'affaires ne voulait pas un billet d'avion. L'audio dit qu'il 'a exigé qu'on paie un taxi pour l'emmener à Manchester'. Il était furieux car on ne pouvait offrir qu'un siège chaud et un horaire.
explanation.es: INCORRECTO. El empresario no quería un billete de avión. El audio dice que 'exigió que pagáramos un taxi para llevarlo a Manchester'. Estaba furioso porque solo le ofrecieron 'un asiento caliente y un horario del tren de la mañana'.
explanation.ru: НЕПРАВИЛЬНО. Бизнесмен не хотел билет на самолёт. В аудио говорится, что он 'потребовал оплатить такси до Манчестера'. Он разозлился, потому что ему предложили лишь 'тёплое место и расписание утреннего поезда'.
